title: Zeros of polynomials via matrix theory and continued fractions
speaker: Olga Holtz
affiliation: University of California, Berkeley; Member, School of Mathematics
abstract: After a brief review of various classical connections between problems of polynomial zero localization, continued fractions, and matrix theory, I will show a few ways to generalize these classical techniques to get new results about some interesting polynomials and entire functions.

proof_stages.label: Classical sign-change zero localization
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins from Descartes' rule and Sturm's theorem, fixing strong sign changes as the basic count that will control real-zero localization.

proof_stages.label: Euclidean algorithm, continued fractions, and generalized Jacobi matrices
proof_stages.summary: A flexible polynomial Euclidean algorithm produces continued fractions and a generalized Jacobi matrix whose principal minors encode the polynomial remainders and their ratios.

proof_stages.label: Sturm sequence as Cauchy-index bookkeeping
proof_stages.summary: Changing the sign convention in the Euclidean algorithm gives Sturm sequences, whose sign-change jumps at poles compute Cauchy indices and hence real-root counts on intervals or on the whole line.

proof_stages.label: Hankel and Hurwitz determinant criteria for stability
proof_stages.summary: Expansions at infinity lead to Hankel determinant sign-change formulae, while Hurwitz matrices built from even/odd polynomial parts yield the Hermite-Biehler/Routh-Hurwitz stability criterion and its Stieltjes continued fraction form.

proof_stages.label: Total nonnegativity via matrix factorization
proof_stages.summary: The total nonnegativity of Hurwitz matrices is explained by factoring out common-factor Toeplitz pieces and Stieltjes continued-fraction factors, then applying Schoenberg and Cauchy-Binet.

proof_stages.label: Relaxed pole-sign conditions and self-interlacing classes
proof_stages.summary: Dropping the requirement that all partial-fraction poles be negative leads to new zero-interlacing classes characterized by positive residues, arbitrary real pole parameters, determinant formulae, and sign-change counts.

proof_stages.label: Derivative Hurwitz matrix test for real negative zeros
proof_stages.summary: Replacing the even/odd split by the pair p and p′ gives a Hurwitz-type matrix criterion that, at least theoretically, detects when all zeros are real and negative.

proof_stages.label: m-fold splitting and braided Euclidean sector exclusion
proof_stages.summary: Splitting a polynomial into residue classes modulo m leads to a braided Euclidean algorithm whose positive leading coefficients force zeros to avoid a sector of total angle 2π/m, though unlike the classical m=2 case the converse fails.

examples.name: Matrix ODE asymptotic stability
examples.note: The matrix differential equation motivation explains why counting polynomial roots in the left half-plane is useful, but it does not supply any step of the determinant or continued-fraction criteria.
examples.engagement_reason: matrix ODE asymptotic stability is cited only as motivation for left-half-plane eigenvalue/root criteria, with no concrete system analyzed

passes.reason: A matrix ODE with spectrum in the left half-plane is used as ordinary motivation for Hurwitz stability, not as a sporadic, limiting, symmetric, or refuting example.

examples.name: Self-interlacing extreme case
examples.note: The case where half the roots are negative and half positive makes the relaxed-sign class concrete by showing that f(z) and f(-z) interlace.
examples.engagement_reason: the self-interlacing extreme case is described and characterized, but no specific polynomial or construction is worked through

passes.reason: The self-interlacing case is explicitly the extreme case where the negative and positive roots split evenly and interlace under z -> -z.

examples.name: The m=2 half-plane specialization
examples.note: The speaker notes that when m=2 the sector theorem reduces to the Routh-Hurwitz half-plane situation, serving as a consistency check rather than a proof step.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker specializes the general m-sector result to m=2 and identifies that the forbidden sector becomes a half-plane, recovering Routh-Hurwitz

passes.reason: The m=2 case is the minimal/base specialization of the m-part construction and gives the maximal sector angle, namely a half-plane.

examples.name: Lee-Yang zeros
examples.note: The Lee-Yang reference is a brief application-oriented reminder that zero localization on lines or circles matters outside the immediate polynomial-matrix framework.
examples.engagement_reason: Lee-Yang zeros are only invoked as an example of why zeros on lines or circles are interesting, with no analysis performed
